questions.question: Explain the difference between point source and non-point source water pollution with examples.
keyPoints: Point sources are identifiable single discharge points (pipes, outlets) e.g., industrial effluent pipes, sewage treatment plant outfalls
Non-point sources are diffuse, untraceable origins e.g., agricultural runoff, urban stormwater, atmospheric deposition
Key difference: point sources can be monitored and controlled at specific locations; non-point sources are harder to manage

questions.question: What is Eutrophication? List its causes and effects on aquatic ecosystems.
keyPoints: Eutrophication: excessive nutrient enrichment causing accelerated plant/algae growth
Causes: agricultural fertilizer runoff (N, P), sewage discharge, industrial effluent
Effects: algae blooms block sunlight → plants die → bacterial decomposition depletes oxygen → fish kill → dead zones

questions.question: Define BOD and COD. How are they used in assessing water quality?
keyPoints: BOD: Biochemical Oxygen Demand - oxygen needed by microorganisms to decompose organic matter
COD: Chemical Oxygen Demand - oxygen needed to chemically oxidize all organic matter
Higher BOD/COD indicates more pollution; used to determine pollution load, treatment requirements, and receiving water capacity

questions.question: List four major water pollutants and describe their effects on human health.
keyPoints: Biological (bacteria, viruses): causes waterborne diseases like cholera, typhoid, diarrhea
Heavy metals (lead, mercury): neurological damage, kidney failure, cancer
Pesticides: endocrine disruption, reproductive problems, cancer
Nutrients (nitrates): methemoglobinemia (blue baby syndrome) in infants

questions.question: Explain the process of biomagnification with an example.
keyPoints: Biomagnification: increasing concentration of persistent pollutants up the food chain
Example: Mercury in water → plankton absorb it → small fish eat plankton → larger fish eat small fish → humans at top have highest concentration
Process occurs because pollutants are not metabolized/excreted and accumulate in fatty tissues

questions.question: What is thermal pollution? Discuss its effects on aquatic life.
keyPoints: Thermal pollution: unnatural warming of water bodies from industrial cooling, power plants

Effects: reduced dissolved oxygen (warm water holds less O2); metabolic rates of aquatic organisms increase; species sensitive to temperature changes die; shifts in species composition toward warm-water species

questions.question: Describe the three stages of wastewater treatment.
keyPoints: Primary treatment: physical removal of settleable solids and floating materials through screening and sedimentation
Secondary treatment: biological process where microorganisms decompose remaining organic matter (activated sludge, trickling filters)
Tertiary treatment: advanced treatment to remove nutrients (N, P), pathogens, and trace contaminants (filtration, disinfection)

questions.question: Discuss the major sources of water pollution in India. Suggest measures to control water pollution.
keyPoints: Sources: industrial effluents (heavy metals, chemicals), domestic sewage (most untreated), agricultural runoff (fertilizers, pesticides), mining activities, solid waste disposal
Effects: waterborne diseases, ecosystem destruction, contamination of groundwater
Control measures: stricter enforcement of Water Act 1974, building sewage treatment plants, promoting industrial recycling, integrated nutrient management, public awareness

questions.question: Explain the nitrogen cycle and how human activities have disrupted it, leading to environmental problems.
keyPoints: Natural nitrogen cycle: fixation (bacteria convert N2 to ammonia), nitrification (ammonia to nitrates), assimilation (plants absorb nitrates), denitrification (nitrates back to N2)
Human disruption: industrial nitrogen fixation (fertilizers), fossil fuel burning (NOx), sewage discharge
Consequences: excess nitrates cause eutrophication, groundwater contamination (methemoglobinemia), acid rain, climate change (N2O is greenhouse gas)

questions.question: What are heavy metals as water pollutants? Discuss their sources, effects, and control measures.
keyPoints: Heavy metals: lead, mercury, cadmium, arsenic, chromium - toxic metallic elements
Sources: industrial processes, mining, batteries, paints, agricultural pesticides
Effects: bioaccumulation, neurological damage (lead, mercury), kidney damage (cadmium), cancer (arsenic)
Control measures: proper waste disposal, industrial wastewater treatment, monitoring of contaminated sites, regulations on industrial emissions

questions.question: Discuss the impact of water pollution on aquatic ecosystems and biodiversity.
keyPoints: Oxygen depletion: organic pollution reduces DO, causing fish kills and species decline
Habitat destruction: eutrophication destroys aquatic plant habitats
Bioaccumulation: toxins concentrate up food chain, affecting top predators
Species composition changes: pollution-tolerant species replace sensitive ones, reducing biodiversity
Acidification: affects shell formation in marine organisms, disrupts food webs
